beats.prompt: Two new patterns, both length 3: THH and HTH. One waits longer on a fair coin. Which?

THH waits longer.note: Close, but it's the other way — let's build both and see.

HTH waits longer.note: Good hunch — HTH waits 10 vs THH's 8. We'll show why.

Same — both length 3.note: Same length, but not the same wait — structure decides it.

hints: No wrong guess yet — commit and we'll derive both.
Length is equal; self-overlap is not.
HTH waits longer (10 vs 8).

beats.prompt: Here are the two patterns we'll build: THH and HTH. Review the compare cards, then continue.

feedback.correct: THH vs HTH — let's build them from scratch.
hints: Both length 3.
We'll derive each wait.
Continue.

beats.prompt: Read what self-overlap means, then continue to find HTH's overlaps.

interaction.title: Overlap = self-similarity
interaction.body: A pattern "overlaps itself" when its start also appears at its end. Those overlaps are the only reason two same-length patterns can wait different amounts. We'll find them by sliding the pattern over itself.

hints: Start = end?
That's a self-overlap.
Continue.

beats.prompt: Press Flip and watch the state chip advance toward THH. Keep flipping until THH appears and you see a near-miss.

feedback.correct: Notice how a stray flip can knock you back a step, not all the way.
hints: Flip and watch the chip climb ∅ → T → TH → THH.
A bad flip falls back to a shorter match, not always to ∅.
Continue once THH lands.

beats.prompt: Tap each row to slide HTH over itself and check for a match where the start reappears at the end. An overlap of length L adds 2^L flips (2 to the power L) to the expected wait.
beats.interviewNote: Sliding a pattern over itself is the autocorrelation / Guibas–Odlyzko correlation set; the border lengths are exactly the overlaps that set the expected wait.

feedback.correct: HTH overlaps at lengths 1 and 3 — that extra length-1 overlap is the source of the extra flips.
hints: Check each shift for a start-equals-end overlap.
HTH matches at length 3 (itself) and length 1 (the H).

beats.prompt: Optional: why each overlap is worth 2^L.

interaction.title: Why an overlap of length L is worth 2^L
interaction.body: A fresh $1 bettor steps in before every flip, each betting the pattern begins right now. A fair coin doubles a winning bet, so a bettor who has called L flips correctly is holding 2^L. The moment the pattern lands, the only bettors still holding money are the ones whose calls match the pattern's start and its end at once — exactly the self-overlaps the ruler finds. Each overlap of length L leaves one such survivor with 2^L, and because every bet is fair, the average wait equals the cash on the table: HTH's overlaps of length 1 and 3 give 2¹ + 2³ = 2 + 8 = 10.

feedback.correct: Each overlap = one fair bet still alive = 2^L on the table.
hints: A fair coin doubles a winning bet.
After L correct flips, a bettor holds 2^L.
Survivors are exactly your overlaps — sum the 2^L.

beats.prompt: On the THH graph, tap the destination state for each outcome — H and T — then press Check.

feedback.correct: Right — from TH, an H completes THH, but a T falls back to E1 (you still have a T), not all the way to ∅.
hints: From TH, which flip finishes THH?
A tail isn't a total reset here — you still hold a T.
E2 on H → done; E2 on T → E1.

beats.prompt: Tap the tiles into the slots to complete the wait-time equations for THH. E₀ is worked out — complete E₁ and E₂.

copy.workedExplanation: From E₀ (no progress) one flip costs 1. Heads leaves you at E₀ (THH needs a T first); tails advances to E₁ (you've matched the T).

wrong-var-generic: From a matched prefix, heads and tails lead to different states — trace each.
One flip extends the match toward THH; the other falls back to a shorter matched prefix (not always ∅).

feedback.correct: That's the THH system — solving it gives E₀ = 8.
hints: Every live state costs 1 per flip.
Split each row into the heads branch and the tails branch.
E₁ = 1 + ½·E₂ + ½·E₁; E₂ = 1 + ½·E₃ + ½·E₁.

beats.prompt: You built THH's equations and found HTH's overlaps. Type each pattern's average wait.

feedback.correct: THH = 8, HTH = 10 — the extra overlap costs HTH 2 more flips.
hints: THH has only the trivial overlap.
HTH has an extra length-1 overlap.
8 and 10.

beats.prompt: Run the simulation for THH. Does the empirical mean settle on 8?

feedback.correct: It converges to 8 — theory and simulation agree.
hints: Run a batch.
Watch the running mean settle.
It lands near 8.

beats.prompt: Each tile is one of HTH's overlaps. Tap each tile to add its 2^L value and build HTH's wait.

feedback.correct: 2¹ + 2³ = 2 + 8 = 10 — the border sum is the wait.
hints: One chip per overlap you found.
Lengths 1 and 3 → 2 and 8.
Add them: 10.

beats.prompt: What makes HTH wait longer? Match each pattern to how it recovers from a near-miss.

feedback.correct: Exactly — HTH's extra length-1 overlap lets a near-miss keep progress, which (paradoxically) lengthens the wait.
hints: Look at the near-miss edges side by side.
One pattern preserves a matched head; the other resets.
HTH's extra overlap is the difference.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: use the overlap shortcut to find E[HTHT] - the average flips until HTHT first appears. Type a whole number.

interaction.scenario: Length 4 doesn't automatically mean \(2^4 = 16\) — slide \(\text{HTHT}\) over itself and watch for a near-miss that keeps progress.

feedback.correct: \(E[\text{HTHT}] = 20\). Use the overlap shortcut: \[ E[P] = \sum 2^L \] summed over every self-overlap length \(L\). Slide \(\text{HTHT}\) over itself: the full shift (length 4) always matches, and at shift 2 the leading \(\text{HT}\) aligns with the trailing \(\text{HT}\), giving overlaps at lengths 2 and 4. Substituting: \[ E[\text{HTHT}] = 2^2 + 2^4 = 4 + 16 = 20. \] Missing the hidden length-2 overlap gives \(16\) — the wait for \(\text{HHTT}\), which has only the full-length match — but \(\text{HTHT}\) waits 4 flips longer because that extra overlap lets near-misses preserve partial progress.
hints: Longer doesn't automatically mean a longer wait. Slide \(\text{HTHT}\) over itself — where does its start line up with its end?
The full length always overlaps. Check every shift for a start-equals-end match. The overlap shortcut: \(E[P] = \sum 2^L\).
Overlaps at length 2 (\(\text{HT} = \text{HT}\)) and length 4: \(2^2 + 2^4 = 4 + 16 = 20\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to see what you transferred to brand-new patterns: THH = 8, HTH = 10.

feedback.correct: You built, solved, and overlap-checked two unseen patterns: THH = 8, HTH = 10.
hints: Same toolkit, new patterns.
Overlap sets the extra wait.
Done.
